message.content:
I need to understand how AOSP zygote works and how signal handlers are inherited by child processes.

Research focus:
1. How zygote fork/exec works in AOSP (Android Runtime)
2. When a process is forked from zygote, are signal handlers inherited? 
3. How does the crash handler in system_server/zygote handle signals?
4. Look for how libCrashDump or debug logs handle signals in AOSP

Search in these areas if they exist locally:
- Any AOSP platform source code
- Any references to zygote in the penguin.opencore codebase
- Any documentation about Android signal handling

Provide details on the mechanism of signal handler inheritance across fork(), and any gotchas.

Code compiled with NDK doesn’t run in a VM and can do wild things otherwise impossible to achieve. One thing commonly not that straightforward to achieve in native code is crash handling. Then create an activity with a single button in the center. Our button will trigger a crash to showcase our crash handler. */ public native void deinitSignalHandler(); /** * A native method that is implemented by the 'native-lib' native library, * which is packaged with this application. It will throw a C++ exception * and catch it in the signal handler which will be visible in the logs. For this new `MainActivity` to work as expected, we must implement the underlying native code in a language compatible with JNI. log-lib # Specifies the name of the NDK library that # you want CMake to locate. native-lib # Links the target library to the log library # included in the NDK.","score":0.18889222,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://docs.sentry.io/platforms/native/advanced-usage/signal-handling/","title":"Signal Handling | Sentry for Native","content":"# Signal Handling. The Native SDK uses signal handling on all supported platforms. On Linux (and Android), it is the primary mechanism to detect errors. A (POSIX) signal is a way for an operating system kernel to inform an application about an exceptional event and provide a mechanism for the application to react to that event. This document provides guidance on signal handling in the Native SDK. * If you want to act on any crash and not consider specific signals, register an `on_crash()` hook instead of registering a signal handler. In the context of the Native SDK, we are only interested in signals that will result in the application's unexpected termination (crashes). Further, it is crucial to understand that this signal handler stack configuration affects only a single thread and that the Native SDK on Linux can only initialize the stack for the thread on which you called `sentry_init()`.","score":0.16885726,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://access.redhat.com/articles/2921161","title":"Using the fork function in signal handlers - Red Hat Customer Portal","content":"An out-of-process crash handler preserves more process information (such as register and stack contents at the time of the crash) compared to an","score":0.15949243,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://medium.com/@esmaeeilmoradi700/decoding-the-android-kernel-6f70c94c0794","title":"Decoding the Android Kernel","content":"An Expert’s Guide to Zygote, Binder… | by EsmaeeilMoradi | Medium. ## An Expert’s Guide to Zygote, Binder IPC, and 16KB Pages. This report is prepared with the goal of providing a comprehensive and authoritative understanding of the Linux Kernel layers that underpin the Android platform. A deep comprehension of how the Kernel manages resources — especially when facing memory bottlenecks and the complexities of **Inter-Process Communication (IPC)** — is a vital and non-negotiable principle for all architectural decisions. The Android platform fundamentally relies on the proven capabilities of the Linux Kernel, particularly in the critical areas of **Security**, **Resource Management**, and **Hardware Abstraction**. This reliance is not merely historical; it is a **strategic architectural decision** that enables the Kernel to function as a **Decisive Executor** operating at the highest level of privilege. Kernel Space and User Space Separation**. This critical separation is enforced at the level of the CPU’s architecture by the Memory Management Unit (MMU) and the **Protection Rings**:.","score":0.13738221,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://stackoverflow.com/questions/26309046/android-native-code-fork-has-issues-with-ipc-binder","title":"Android native code fork() has issues with IPC/Binder - Stack Overflow","content":"The problem is that fork() only clones the current thread, not all threads. In the new process, the Binder IPC code will expect the Binder helper threads to be","score":0.09881625,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/92747/child-process-does-not-inherit-the-pending-signals-from-the-parent-after-a-fork","title":"child process does not inherit the pending signals from the parent ...","content":"A \"pending\" signal is either one which simply hasn't been delivered yet, or one which has been blocked temporarily by the receiving process.","score":0.057774507,"raw_content":null}],"response_time":0.68,"request_id":"3b9c500f-48a4-4bc9-b148-849163271ed1"}

content.thinking:
I now have good web search results. Let me compile a comprehensive answer about:

1. How zygote fork/exec works in AOSP
2. Signal handler inheritance across fork()
3. Crash handler in system_server/zygote
4. libCrashDump signal handling

Key findings from the search:
1. AOSP official docs: https://source.android.com/docs/core/runtime/zygote
2. The key file: core/jni/com_android_internal_os_Zygote.cpp
3. Signal handlers ARE inherited after fork() but NOT after exec()
4. SIGCHLD handling in zygote
5. Signal handlers are reset to default action during execve() because the handler code may have disappeared

Let me look for more specific content about:
- How zygote handles SIGCHLD
- The crash dump mechanism
- libCrashDump if it exists in AOSP

Let me search for more details on these specific topics.

input.query: Android zygote SIGCHLD signal handler system_server crash dump
Android libCrashDump signal handler implementation AOSP
Android signal handler reset execve fork inheritance POSIX behavior

We noticed that sometimes (rarely) after a device reboot, we face continuous ANR of the system process (every 2 or 3 minutes) for a duration between 30 minutes to several hours then, it suddendly ends like it begins. `02-09 09:05:19.583 3348 3363 W ActivityManager: Timeout executing service: ServiceRecord{e71df53 u0 android/com.android.internal.backup.LocalTransportService}. 02-09 09:05:20.217 3925 3939 I SubTitleServic: Thread[3,tid=3939,WaitingInMainSignalCatcherLoop,Thread*=0x7331139000,peer=0x13440020,\"Signal Catcher\"]: reacting to signal 3. 02-09 09:05:20.217 3925 3939 I SubTitleServic:. 02-09 09:05:20.262 3925 3939 I SubTitleServic: Wrote stack traces to '[tombstoned]'. 02-09 09:05:20.264 3916 3924 I com.android.se: Thread[3,tid=3924,WaitingInMainSignalCatcherLoop,Thread*=0x7331139000,peer=0x13380020,\"Signal Catcher\"]: reacting to signal 3. 02-09 09:05:20.264 3916 3924 I com.android.se:. 02-09 09:05:20.312 3916 3924 I com.android.se: Wrote stack traces to '[tombstoned]'. 02-09 09:05:23.622 3348 3363 E ActivityManager: 0.4% 3092/hdmicecd: 0.1% user + 0.3% kernel. 02-09 09:05:23.622 3348 3363 E ActivityManager: 0.1% 3095/systemcontrol: 0% user + 0.1% kernel.","score":0.042087726,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://newandroidbook.com/Book/servs","title":"Chapter 4 - Runtime Services","content":"The rc also defines a \"late\\_start\" class, for services which depend on the /data partition, though no default services belong to it. In this section, we adopt the service class division, but - since most services are in \"main\" - further subcategorize by function. We begin with a discussion of init, which is the very first process to launch (PID 1), as thus serves as the progenitor of all user mode processes. The Android init is different than that of Linux, with the most important differences being in its support of System Properties and using a particular set of rc files. As it so happens, `init` also fills additional roles - assuming the guise of ueventd and watchdogd, two important core services which are also implemented by init, loaded through a symbolic link. All other services are generally classified into the \"main\" category, so a subcategorization by Network Services (netd, mdnsd, mtpd and rild), and Graphics and Media Services (surfaceflinger, bootanimation, mediaserver and drmserver) follows.","score":0.00042804275,"raw_content":null}],"response_time":0.69,"request_id":"f536e3d3-a126-4b6d-a72b-0ecaab281f30"}

content.thinking: Let me fetch the actual AOSP source code for the Zygote implementation to get more detailed technical information.

input.input: Explain in detail how Android zygote fork works: 1) The zygote process initialization and signal handler setup 2) How fork() inherits signal handlers to child processes 3) What happens to signal handlers after execve() 4) How system_server handles SIGCHLD 5) The UnsetSigChldHandler function and its role 6) How debuggerd/crashdump catches signals

content.content:
{"content":"# Android Zygote, fork, exec, and Unix signal semantics — technical report

This report synthesizes verified AOSP, Bionic, Linux manpages, and selected secondary-source findings to explain how Android’s zygote-based fork model interacts with Unix signal semantics. Each factual paragraph cites its supporting sources. Where the available evidence is silent, an Evidence Gaps section lists what could not be answered from the provided material.

## Executive summary
- Zygote is the central process that initializes runtime state and then forks to create both system_server and application processes; the Java side (ZygoteInit) calls native initialization that opens the zygote control socket and performs the native fork/specialize logic [1], [2], [5], [7].  
- Zygote-family native code historically installs a SIGCHLD handler to reap children and to detect system_server death; implementation and exact placement evolved across releases (examples documented in Android 6.0 and 7.1 sources and in older Dalvik code) [3], [6], [15].  
- POSIX/Linux semantics apply for inheritance across fork (signal handlers are inherited, pending signals are not) and for execve (caught handlers reset to default; SIG_IGN is preserved) with Bionic implementing the standard libc interfaces and exposing helper functions that affect SA_RESTART and handler setting [16], [17], [18], [11], [13].  
- Android’s init uses a signalfd-based SIGCHLD handling design (SA_NOCLDSTOP + signalfd/epoll) rather than a plain async handler, showing that multiple Android components adopt different approaches for SIGCHLD delivery and reaping [8], [9], [10].  
- Debugger/tombstone generation on Android is integrated with Bionic’s signal handling hooks and debuggerd; Bionic installs signal hooks that interact with debuggerd to generate tombstones and debuggerd’s handler code processes special internal signals (e.g., BIONIC_SIGNAL_DEBUGGER) and writes tombstones under /data/tombstones/ [11], [12], [19].

References for every quoted source are at the end.

---

## 1) Zygote initialization: native and Java sides (what runs, where)
- Zygote’s Java entrypoint: ZygoteInit.main (invoked by app_process when the process is started as a zygote) arranges runtime initialization (RuntimeInit.redirectLogStreams(), RuntimeInit.commonInit()), then calls a native initializer via a private native method nativeZygoteInit(), and finally performs child startup via RuntimeInit.applicationInit when executing a child’s main; ZygoteInit recognizes arguments such as "start-system-server" and constructs system‑server arguments like --setuid, --setgid, --setgroups, --capabilities, and --nice-name [1], [4]. [1], [4]

- Native-side zygote control: the native Zygote implementation (com_android_internal_os_Zygote.cpp) performs control-socket acquisition for the zygote (android_get_control_socket name "zygote" or "zygote_secondary"), registers JNI hooks (callPostForkSystemServerHooks, callPostForkChildHooks, etc.), and stores the system server pid in a native gSystemServerPid variable used by native monitoring logic [2]. The app_process startup path (app_main.cpp) arranges to invoke the ZygoteInit Java class when the process is started with --zygote, and app_main.cpp’s onZygoteInit starts the Binder thread pool with ProcessState::self()->startThreadPool() for zygote children [5]. [2], [5]

- What this implies for reproduction/inspection: inspect these files to follow initialization and native handoff:
  - Java: frameworks/base/core/java/com/android/internal/os/ZygoteInit.java [1]
  - Native JNI glue: frameworks/base/core/jni/com_android_internal_os_Zygote.cpp (master) [2]
  - app_process entry: frameworks/base/cmds/app_process/app_main.cpp [5]
  - Android documentation overview of the zygote role: source.android.com runtime/zygote [7]  
  See the References section for direct links. [1], [2], [5], [7]

## 2) Which signal handlers zygote installs at startup (names, dispositions, SA_* flags)
- Where the zygote SIGCHLD handler appears in AOSP sources: multiple zygote implementations (historical Dalvik and newer frameworks native code) install a SIGCHLD handler via sigaction. Examples include Dalvik’s native zygote (dalvik_system_Zygote.cpp) that sets a SIGCHLD handler with sa_handler = sigchldHandler (no additional SA flags in that file’s setSignalHandler) and that restores SIG_DFL in unsetSignalHandler [6]. The frameworks/native Zygote native code for specific Android tags (e.g., android-6.0.0_r1 and android-7.1.2_r39) includes SetSigChldHandler which installs a SigChldHandler via sigaction and UnsetSigChldHandler which resets SIGCHLD to SIG_DFL via sigaction; the Android 7.1 tag additionally shows that SetSigChldHandler temporarily blocks SIGCHLD during forks by building a sigset containing SIGCHLD [15], [6], [3]. [6], [15], [3]

- Handler behavior documented in code:
  - The zygote SIGCHLD handlers call waitpid(-1, &status, WNOHANG) in a loop to reap exited children, check WCOREDUMP(status) to detect core dumps and log that condition, and may take action if the terminated pid matches the stored system-server pid (e.g., kill the zygote or abort) [3], [6]. [3], [6]

- SA_* flags: examples in the Android code show that SetSigChldHandler uses sigaction; Dalvik’s setSignalHandler zeroes the sigaction structure before setting the handler (implying default SA_* flags unless explicitly set) [6], [15]. The code paths that temporarily block SIGCHLD around fork use sigprocmask/sigaddset to block/unblock the signal during critical regions [15]. [6], [15]

- Practical pointers (where to look in source tree):
  - frameworks/base/core/jni/com_android_internal_os_Zygote.cpp (master and branch tags such as android-6.0.0_r1 and android-7.1.2_r39 show SetSigChldHandler/UnsetSigChldHandler implementations) [2], [3], [15]
  - dalvik/vm/native/dalvik_system_Zygote.cpp for older Dalvik-era zygote SIGCHLD code [6]

(References for the above file-level facts are [6], [3], [15], [2].)

## 3) How fork() inherits signal handlers on Android (kernel/libc semantics and Bionic specifics)
- POSIX/Linux kernel semantics relevant to fork and signal handlers: on Linux (POSIX conformant), signal handlers set by the process are inherited across fork (i.e., the child inherits the parent’s signal dispositions) while pending signals are not inherited by the child; fork creates a new process with its own signal handling state copied from the parent [16], [17]. The Linux sigaction semantics and the SA_* flags behavior (e.g., SA_NOCLDSTOP prevents SIGCHLD for stop/continue events; SA_NOCLDWAIT can prevent children from becoming zombies while SIGCHLD may still be generated on Linux) are recorded in the sigaction manual [16]. [16], [17]

- Bionic libc specifics shown in sources:
  - Bionic provides the expected POSIX signal manipulation functions: sigignore uses sigaction64 to set a handler to SIG_IGN, siginterrupt toggles SA_RESTART for a signal (clearing SA_RESTART when flag is non-zero, setting it otherwise), and other wrappers adapt BSD-style masks to POSIX via sigprocmask where appropriate [11]. Bionic’s implementation detail confirms that signals and SA flags are handled via the standard sigaction/sigprocmask interfaces in libc, i.e., there are no documented anomalous forks semantics in Bionic itself beyond the POSIX/Linux model in the referenced implementation [11]. [11]

- Behavior of specific flags across fork (as supported by the evidence):
  - Handlers (user-defined via sigaction) are copied to the child after fork (inheritance) — this is consistent with the general fork semantics [17].  
  - SA_RESTART is a handler-level flag which Bionic can toggle (siginterrupt) and is part of the handler state that the child inherits because the handler is inherited [11], [16].  
  - SA_NOCLDSTOP and SA_NOCLDWAIT are sigaction flags affecting SIGCHLD delivery/behavior; their semantics (SA_NOCLDSTOP prevents SIGCHLD on stop/continue, SA_NOCLDWAIT prevents zombies on Linux though SIGCHLD may still be delivered) are those documented for the kernel/libc [16]. [16]

- Summary: The evidence shows standard POSIX/Linux semantics apply on Android, with Bionic implementing the standard functions (sigaction, sigprocmask, siginterrupt, sigignore) used to set/modify dispositions and SA_* flags; children inherit the parent’s handler dispositions and flags at fork, while pending signals are not inherited. [11], [16], [17]

## 4) What happens to signal dispositions across execve()
- execve(2) semantics documented in the manual: execve preserves dispositions that are SIG_IGN and leaves SIG_DFL as default for signals that had been SIG_DFL, but it resets the disposition of any signal that was caught (i.e., had a user-defined handler) to SIG_DFL; POSIX/Linux behavior for SIG_IGN is that ignored dispositions are left unchanged across execve on Linux (POSIX permits implementations to reset them, but the Linux behavior is to leave SIG_IGN unchanged) [18]. [18]

- Bionic-level notes relevant to exec: Bionic’s exec path (execvpe → execve) follows the standard execve behavior, and also contains code handling scripts (EN0EXEC → __exec_as_script) and PATH lookup; the referenced Bionic exec implementation confirms that execve is the final syscall invoked by libc wrappers, so execve’s kernel semantics determine disposition changes at exec time [13]. [13]

- Practical implications for Android processes:
  - If a signal was set to SIG_IGN in the parent, it will remain SIG_IGN after execve on Linux as per execve(2) documentation; user-installed handlers (non-SIG_IGN) will be reset to SIG_DFL by the kernel on execve [18], [13]. [18], [13]

## 5) How system_server / the zygote family handle SIGCHLD in practice (reaping, logging, interactions)
- Zygote’s SIGCHLD handler behavior (examples in tags):
  - Android 6.0 Zygote code sets a SIGCHLD handler before forking; the SIGCHLD handler loops with waitpid(-1, &status, WNOHANG), logs process exit information (including detection of core dumps), and contains logic to treat system_server death specially (e.g., logging that the system server process has been created or died and taking restart/abort actions) [3]. The handler logs core-dump events via WCOREDUMP(status) checks and may call RuntimeAbort or kill the zygote process in response to system-server termination [3]. [3]

- Historical Dalvik implementation:
  - dalvik_system_Zygote.cpp implements setSignalHandler (configuring a SIGCHLD handler via sigaction) and unsetSignalHandler (restoring SIGCHLD to SIG_DFL via sigaction), with the SIGCHLD handler logging core dumps and killing the zygote if the system-server pid exited; error paths log warnings on waitpid errors [6]. [6]

- SetSigChldHandler / UnsetSigChldHandler in frameworks native code:
  - frameworks/base native Zygote code (variants across tags) provides SetSigChldHandler to install a SigChldHandler (via sigaction) and UnsetSigChldHandler to reset SIGCHLD to SIG_DFL; some variants block SIGCHLD temporarily around fork (build a sigset containing SIGCHLD) to avoid races during the fork/child-specialize window [15]. [15]

- Init’s different approach:
  - Android’s init uses a signalfd + epoll approach for SIGCHLD: init’s signal handling code installs SIGCHLD with sa_handler = SIGCHLD_handler and sa_flags = SA_NOCLDSTOP, writes an indicator byte into a socketpair from the async handler, and services it via epoll to perform reaping/handling in event-loop context; this demonstrates an alternate pattern (signalfd/epoll) used by a core Android component for SIGCHLD processing [8], [9], [10]. [8], [9], [10]

- Interaction notes:
  - The documented zygote SIGCHLD handlers actively reap child processes with waitpid(-1, &status, WNOHANG) loops and log/act on core dumps or system_server death; init’s signalfd design prevents SIGCHLD delivery on child stop/continue events (SA_NOCLDSTOP) and handles signal handling in an epoll-driven loop [3], [6], [15], [8], [9], [10]. [3], [6], [15], [8], [9], [10]

## 6) The UnsetSigChldHandler function — where, what, and why
- Definitions in AOSP:
  - UnsetSigChldHandler (or unsetSignalHandler in older Dalvik code) is defined in the native zygote implementation files (examples: dalvik_system_Zygote.cpp for Dalvik-era code and frameworks/base/core/jni/com_android_internal_os_Zygote.cpp in framework natives) and performs a sigaction call to restore the SIGCHLD disposition to SIG_DFL; the Dalvik implementation logs on error when restoring the disposition [6], [15]. [6], [15]

- When/why it is used (evidence-supported statements only):
  - Sources show that zygote/native code explicitly installs a SIGCHLD handler (SetSigChldHandler) and provides an UnsetSigChldHandler that restores the default disposition; SetSigChldHandler sometimes blocks SIGCHLD around fork operations to avoid races [15], [6]. The presence of an Unset function indicates code paths where zygote-native logic intentionally removes its SIGCHLD handler (restores SIG_DFL) — e.g., older Dalvik code provides unsetSignalHandler — but the provided evidence does not enumerate the complete set of call sites or the exact runtime moment(s) in which UnsetSigChldHandler is invoked during zygote lifecycle transitions. [6], [15]

- Effect on reaping / crash handling:
  - Restoring SIGCHLD to SIG_DFL via UnsetSigChldHandler leaves the process with the default disposition for child-termination signals; if no other code installs a handler or uses signalfd, child processes that exit could become zombies unless some other reaper (e.g., init or another designated parent) performs wait/waitpid reaping. The referenced zygote handlers that remain installed (when not unset) actively reap children using waitpid with WNOHANG, so unsetting that handler removes that reaping behavior from the process [6], [15], [3]. [6], [15], [3]

## 7) Android crash-handling components (debuggerd, tombstoned): mechanisms and interaction with zygote/system_server signal handling
- Bionic / debuggerd integration:
  - Bionic installs signal hooks designed to communicate with debuggerd so that native crashes can produce tombstones; community-sourced evidence indicates the bionic linker installs debuggerd_signal_handler for several fatal signals and that debuggerd creates tombstone files under /data/tombstones/ when native crashes occur [11]. debuggerd handler code parses siginfo (si_signo, si_code, si_pid, etc.) and logs using async-safe primitives; it also recognizes a BIONIC_SIGNAL_DEBUGGER internal signal used for abort-style handling [19], [11], [12]. [11], [19], [12]

- Sequence of events (evidence-limited, supported steps only):
  - Evidence supports the following observed/control flow facts: Bionic installs a signal-communication hook for crashes that interacts with debuggerd; debuggerd’s handler code processes details from siginfo and writes tombstones under /data/tombstones/ when a native crash is processed [11], [19]. The referenced sources do not provide a fully detailed, step-by-step kernel→user helper or ptrace attachment sequence in the provided material, nor do they document prctl/dumpable flags or core_pattern configuration in the cited facts. [11], [19]  

- Who observes SIGCHLD / who reaps / races:
  - The sources demonstrate that zygote installs SIGCHLD handlers that reap child processes and log core dumps; debuggerd and tombstoned operate via Bionic-installed signal hooks to capture crashes and generate tombstones. The provided evidence does not, however, enumerate authoritative ordering guarantees or the exact race/ordering behavior between zygote/system_server SIGCHLD handlers and debuggerd or tombstoned when an app crashes (for example, which entity observes the SIGCHLD first, whether ptrace attach occurs before SIGCHLD delivery, or the interaction with kernel core dump piping). The evidence therefore supports that multiple components participate (zygote reaping, Bionic/debuggerd tombstone generation) but does not fully describe the interleaving semantics. [3], [6], [11], [19]

## 8) Differences across Android releases and notable changes
- Evolution seen in sources:
  - Older Dalvik-era zygote implementations (dalvik_system_Zygote.cpp) contain explicit setSignalHandler/unsetSignalHandler functions and direct sigaction usage for SIGCHLD [6]. In later framework-native implementations (examples: android-6.0.0_r1 and android-7.1.2_r39 tags of com_android_internal_os_Zygote.cpp), SetSigChldHandler and UnsetSigChldHandler are present, with SetSigChldHandler in some builds temporarily blocking SIGCHLD during fork to avoid races and logging/waitpid behavior to detect core dumps and system_server death [3], [15]. The source.android.com overview documents additional runtime architecture changes (e.g., multiple zygotes on multi-ABI devices, USAP pool on newer devices) that affect forking behavior at a high level but do not in the provided evidence change basic signal semantics [7], [3], [15]. [6], [3], [15], [7]

- Summary of divergence:
  - The primary differences visible in the cited sources are implementation placement and details (Dalvik vs frameworks native), the presence of signalfd for init, and explicit temporary SIGCHLD blocking around forks in some framework implementations. The core POSIX/kernel signal semantics (inheritance across fork; execve resetting caught handlers) remain consistent across referenced artifacts [6], [3], [15], [8], [9], [10], [16], [18]. [6], [3], [15], [8], [9], [10], [16], [18]

## 9) Practical verification steps and code locations to inspect (how to reproduce / trace)
- Source locations to inspect to reproduce/inspect behavior (file + function references):
  - Zygote Java startup: frameworks/base/core/java/com/android/internal/os/ZygoteInit.java — ZygoteInit.zigoteInit and nativeZygoteInit declaration [1].  
  - Zygote native glue: frameworks/base/core/jni/com_android_internal_os_Zygote.cpp — nativeInitNativeState, SetSigChldHandler/UnsetSigChldHandler implementations across tags (see master and release tags) [2], [3], [15].  
  - Dalvik-era zygote SIGCHLD code: dalvik/vm/native/dalvik_system_Zygote.cpp — setSignalHandler, unsetSignalHandler, sigchldHandler [6].  
  - app_process startup: frameworks/base/cmds/app_process/app_main.cpp — onZygoteInit and binder thread pool start [5].  
  - init signal handling: system/core/init/init.cpp and system/core/init/signal_handler.cpp — signalfd setup and SA_NOCLDSTOP behavior [8], [9], [10].  
  - Bionic signal interfaces and exec path: bionic/libc/bionic/signal.cpp (siginterrupt, sigignore, etc.) and bionic/libc/bionic/exec.cpp (exec wrappers) [11], [13].  
  - debuggerd handler: system/core/debuggerd/handler/debuggerd_handler.cpp — signal processing for BIONIC_SIGNAL_DEBUGGER and siginfo extraction [19].  
  Use these files as primary references to reproduce and trace behavior. [1], [2], [5], [6], [8], [9], [10], [11], [13], [19], [3], [15]

- What to look for when tracing at runtime (evidence-backed expectations):
  - Zygote native code logs: earlier tags show Zygote logging messages such as "System server process %d has been created" or that the system server has died and zygote is restarting/aborting; handler logging includes detection of core dumps via WCOREDUMP(status) [3]. [3]  
  - Tombstone presence: debuggerd writes tombstones under /data/tombstones/ when a native crash is processed (as reported in referenced evidence) [11]. [11]  
  - Init behavior: init’s signalfd implementation writes a byte to a socketpair from the async handler and handles it in epoll; the handler uses SA_NOCLDSTOP so stopped/continued children do not trigger reaping signals [9], [8], [10]. [9], [8], [10]

## Evidence Gaps (items not fully specified by the provided sources)
The provided source set documents many relevant implementations and general POSIX semantics, but several requested details are not present in the evidence and therefore cannot be asserted here:

- The precise complete list of all signal handlers the zygote process installs at process start (beyond SIGCHLD) and every SA_* flag set on each signal at the moment of zygote native startup is not enumerated in the cited sources. The sources explicitly show SIGCHLD handling but do not present a full startup instrumentation list. [6], [3], [15]

- Exact call-site list and runtime timing for UnsetSigChldHandler across releases: sources define the function and show its effect (reset SIGCHLD to SIG_DFL) but do not provide a definitive mapping of every runtime moment or branch where UnsetSigChldHandler is invoked during zygote/system_server lifecycle. [6], [15]

- The detailed sequence of kernel→userspace crash-handling steps involving ptrace, core_pattern piping, prctl/dumpable flags, and which process (zygote, debuggerd, init, or system_server) observes which signals first in every crash scenario is not fully documented in the provided evidence. While Bionic/debuggerd integration and tombstone generation are documented, the exact ordering, ptrace attach sequence, and kernel-side core dump helper usage are not present in the cited material. [11], [19]

- A fully version-differentiated matrix (per Android release) listing differences in signal handler installation, Set/Unset behavior, and signal-handling APIs used by zygote/system_server is not present; the evidence provides snapshots for Dalvik-era sources and specific tags (android-6.0.0_r1, android-7.1.2_r39), but not an exhaustive change log across all Android releases. [6], [3], [15], [7]

- Concrete, validated reproducible test programs (C/Java) and exact strace/ptrace command sequences with expected outputs are not present in the supplied evidence; the sources show code locations and expected logs (e.g., zygote/system_server logging and tombstone file outputs) but do not include sample test programs or scripted verification steps. [3], [11], [19]

---

## Recommended inspection and minimal validation plan (sources to read, small checks)
(These steps reference source files above; these actions are supported by the existence of those files and the log behavior described in the cited sources.)
- Inspect the zygote Java and native startup flow:
  - Read frameworks/base/core/java/com/android/internal/os/ZygoteInit.java and frameworks/base/core/jni/com_android_internal_os_Zygote.cpp to follow the Java→native handoff and to locate native functions such as nativeZygoteInit and SetSigChldHandler/UnsetSigChldHandler [1], [2]. [1], [2]

- Inspect zygote-native SIGCHLD implementation and logs:
  - For a release of interest, open the corresponding com_android_internal_os_Zygote.cpp tag (e.g., android-6.0.0_r1, android-7.1.2_r39) to read the SigChldHandler implementation and find the waitpid/WCOREDUMP logic and any system-server pid checks [3], [15]. [3], [15]

- Inspect Dalvik-era code (if relevant):
  - Open dalvik/vm/native/dalvik_system_Zygote.cpp to see setSignalHandler/unsetSignalHandler patterns used historically (useful to compare behavior across runtime stacks) [6]. [6]

- Inspect init’s signalfd-based handling and compare patterns:
  - Read system/core/init/signal_handler.cpp and system/core/init/init.cpp to see a signalfd/epoll approach and SA_NOCLDSTOP usage for SIGCHLD [8], [9], [10]. [8], [9], [10]

- Inspect Bionic’s signal and exec behavior:
  - Read bionic/libc/bionic/signal.cpp and bionic/libc/bionic/exec.cpp to confirm the libc-level primitives (siginterrupt, sigignore) and the exec wrapper behavior [11], [13]. [11], [13]

- Inspect debuggerd/tombstone handling:
  - Read system/core/debuggerd/handler/debuggerd_handler.cpp to see how debuggerd processes signals and extracts siginfo for logging/tombstone generation; check for tombstones under /data/tombstones/ after generating a reproducible native crash (the sources document this tombstone location) [19], [11]. [19], [11]

---

## Conclusion
The provided evidence shows that Android’s zygote model follows standard POSIX/Linux signal semantics with implementation-specific choices in how SIGCHLD is handled and reaped. Zygote-native code (both Dalvik-era and later framework-native implementations) installs a SIGCHLD handler implemented via sigaction and waitpid loops (reaping children and detecting core dumps), and provides functions to unset that handler (restore SIG_DFL). Bionic provides POSIX-compatible signal APIs (sigaction, siginterrupt, sigignore) that determine handler/flag behavior across fork and exec, and integrates with debuggerd to ensure native crash reporting (tombstone creation). Init demonstrates an alternate signalfd/epoll pattern for SIGCHLD delivery which uses SA_NOCLDSTOP. The evidence is explicit about these components’ locations and behaviors, but does not provide exhaustive per-release diffs, full lists of all signals set at zygote startup, nor complete kernel→user crash-handling sequences (ptrace/core_pattern/prctl ordering), which are listed under Evidence Gaps.

---
